section.title: Everlasting Salvation for Zion
section.sectionRationale: This long movement gathers up nearly every thread of the Book of Comfort, Abraham and Sarah as the rock Israel was cut from, the Servant's righteousness as a light to the nations, the awakened arm of the LORD that once split the sea, the cup of wrath finally removed from Zion's hand, and the herald whose feet are beautiful because he announces that God reigns. It ends with a sober command to leave Babylon and carry the LORD's vessels home. It stands as the long crescendo just before the fourth Servant Song.
section.thesis: The LORD who called Abraham from nothing and dried up the sea for the redeemed to cross will again bare His holy arm to comfort Zion, remove the cup of His fury, and send good news of salvation to the ends of the earth, so His people can wake up, put on strength, and leave captivity behind.
section.frameNote: Eight movements: look to Abraham and Sarah; the law and light to the nations; awake, O arm of the LORD; I, even I, am He who comforts you; awake, awake, Jerusalem, the cup removed; awake, awake, clothe yourself, O Zion; how beautiful are the feet who bring good news; and depart, depart, go out from there.

units.label: Look to the rock you were cut from
units.note: Abraham and Sarah, the pattern of God's multiplying grace

verses.text: “Listen to Me, you who pursue righteousness, you who seek the LORD: Look to the rock from which you were cut, and to the quarry from which you were hewn.
Look to Abraham your father, and to Sarah who gave you birth. When I called him, he was but one; then I blessed him and multiplied him.

For the LORD will comfort Zion and will look with compassion on all her ruins; He will make her wilderness like Eden and her desert like the garden of the LORD. Joy and gladness will be found in her, thanksgiving and melodious song.

units.label: A law and a light to the nations

verses.text: Pay attention to Me, My people, and listen to Me, My nation; for a law will go out from Me, and My justice will become a light to the nations; I will bring it about quickly.
My righteousness draws near, My salvation is on the way, and My arms will bring justice to the nations. The islands will look for Me and wait in hope for My arm.

Lift up your eyes to the heavens, and look at the earth below; for the heavens will vanish like smoke, the earth will wear out like a garment, and its people will die like gnats. But My salvation will last forever, and My righteousness will never fail.
Listen to Me, you who know what is right, you people with My law in your hearts: Do not fear the scorn of men; do not be broken by their insults.
For the moth will devour them like a garment, and the worm will eat them like wool. But My righteousness will last forever, My salvation through all generations.”

units.label: Awake, O arm of the LORD
units.note: the God who cut Rahab and dried the sea

verses.text: Awake, awake, put on strength, O {{zeroa|arm}} of the LORD. Wake up as in days past, as in generations of old. Was it not You who cut {{rahab|Rahab}} to pieces, who pierced through the dragon?
Was it not You who dried up the sea, the waters of the great deep, who made a road in the depths of the sea for the redeemed to cross over?
So the redeemed of the LORD will return and enter Zion with singing, crowned with everlasting joy. Gladness and joy will overtake them, and sorrow and sighing will flee.

units.label: I, even I, am He who comforts you

verses.text: “I, even I, am He who comforts you. Why should you be afraid of mortal man, of a son of man who withers like grass?

But you have forgotten the LORD, your Maker, who stretched out the heavens and laid the foundations of the earth. You live in terror all day long because of the fury of the oppressor who is bent on destruction. But where is the fury of the oppressor?
The captive will soon be freed; he will not die in the dungeon, and his bread will not be lacking.
For I am the LORD your God who stirs up the sea so that its waves roar, the LORD of Hosts is His name.
I have put My words in your mouth, and covered you with the shadow of My hand, to establish the heavens, to found the earth, and to say to Zion, ‘You are My people.’”
Awake, awake! Rise up, O Jerusalem, you who have drunk from the hand of the LORD the cup of His fury; you who have drained the goblet to the dregs, the cup that makes men stagger.
Among all the sons she bore, there is no one to guide her; among all the sons she brought up, there is no one to take her hand.
These pairs have befallen you: devastation and destruction, famine and sword. Who will grieve for you? Who can comfort you?
Your sons have fainted; they lie at the head of every street, like an antelope in a net. They are full of the wrath of the LORD, the rebuke of your God.
Therefore now hear this, you afflicted one, drunken, but not with wine.
Thus says your Lord, the LORD, even your God, who defends His people: “See, I have removed from your hand the cup of staggering. From that goblet, the cup of My fury, you will never drink again.
I will place it in the hands of your tormentors, who told you: ‘Lie down, so we can walk over you,’ so that you made your back like the ground, like a street to be traversed.”

units.label: Awake, awake, clothe yourself, O Zion
units.note: sold for nothing, redeemed without money

verses.text: Awake, awake, clothe yourself with strength, O Zion! Put on your garments of splendor, O Jerusalem, holy city! For the uncircumcised and unclean will no longer enter you.
Shake off your dust! Rise up and sit on your throne, O Jerusalem. Remove the chains from your neck, O captive Daughter of Zion.
For this is what the LORD says: “You were sold for nothing, and without money you will be redeemed.”
For this is what the Lord GOD says: “At first My people went down to Egypt to live, then Assyria oppressed them without cause.
And now what have I here? declares the LORD. For My people have been taken without cause; those who rule them taunt, declares the LORD, and My name is blasphemed continually all day long.
Therefore My people will know My name; therefore they will know on that day that I am He who speaks. Here I am!”

units.label: How beautiful are the feet
units.note: good news, peace, salvation, your God reigns

verses.text: How beautiful on the mountains are the feet of those who bring {{besorah|good news}}, who proclaim peace, who bring good tidings, who proclaim salvation, who say to Zion, “Your God reigns!”
Listen! Your watchmen lift up their voices, together they shout for joy. For every eye will see when the LORD returns to Zion.
Break forth in joy, sing together, O ruins of Jerusalem, for the LORD has comforted His people; He has redeemed Jerusalem.
The LORD has bared His holy arm in the sight of all the nations; all the ends of the earth will see the salvation of our God.

units.label: Depart, depart, go out from there
units.note: carry the vessels of the LORD, purified

verses.text: Depart, depart, go out from there! Touch no unclean thing; come out from it, purify yourselves, you who carry the vessels of the LORD.
For you will not leave in a hurry nor flee in haste, for the LORD goes before you, and the God of Israel is your rear guard.

zeroa.gloss: Arm, the picture of God's active, saving strength. Awake, O arm of the LORD calls on the same power that split the sea at the exodus to act again; the same phrase, God's arm bared, returns in 52:10 and 53:1, tying creation, exodus, and the Servant's suffering together as one unfolding display of divine strength.

rahab.gloss: A poetic name for a sea monster or the chaos of the deep, sometimes used for Egypt itself. To cut Rahab to pieces recalls both God's victory over chaos at creation and His defeat of Egypt at the exodus, one image doing double duty as cosmic and historical deliverance.

besorah.gloss: Good news, glad tidings, the root behind the New Testament word for gospel. The herald's beautiful feet bring besorah that God reigns and has redeemed His people; Paul quotes this verse directly in Romans 10:15 of those who preach the good news of Christ.

basic.context: This long section gathers the Book of Comfort's key promises before the climactic fourth Servant Song. Israel in exile is told to remember Abraham, to trust the arm that split the sea, and to know that the cup of God's wrath, which she has drained to the dregs, will finally be removed and placed instead in the hands of her tormentors.
basic.hermeneutics: The repeated cry awake, awake structures the whole passage, first summoning God's arm to act, then Jerusalem to rise from her stupor, then Zion to clothe herself for restoration. It is prophetic poetry announcing a real, historical return from Babylon that also reaches, in language too large for Babylon alone, toward final salvation.
basic.originalLanguages: The zeroa, arm, of the LORD ties creation's defeat of Rahab, the exodus through the sea, and the coming salvation into a single continuous act of divine strength. And the herald's feet are beautiful because they carry besorah, good news, the very word behind gospel, that God reigns.
basic.typology: The arm of the LORD bared for all the nations to see anticipates the arm of the LORD revealed in the Suffering Servant of chapter 53. The herald whose feet are beautiful anticipates every gospel messenger, and supremely Christ Himself, the good news in person.
basic.christ: Paul quotes 52:7 directly in Romans 10:15 of gospel preachers, and applies the phrase how beautiful are the feet to the spread of the good news about Jesus. The bared arm of the LORD in 52:10 leads directly into 53:1, who has believed our message, and to whom has the arm of the LORD been revealed, showing that the ultimate unveiling of God's saving arm is the crucified and risen Christ.
basic.god: God is the covenant-keeping Creator who called one man, Abraham, and multiplied him into a people; He is also the Judge who poured out real wrath on real sin, yet He is the Redeemer who removes that same cup from His people's hand and hands it instead to their oppressors, precisely and only because of what the coming Servant will bear.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 10:15 quotes Isaiah 52:7 of gospel messengers. Galatians 3:6-9 appeals to Abraham as father of all who believe. Isaiah 53:1 continues directly from 52:10 with who has believed our message. Revelation 18 echoes the cup of God's fury given finally to Babylon.
basic.oneStory: From Abraham the one, to the arm that split the sea, to the cup of wrath finally removed and carried instead by another, the whole passage is one long rehearsal of the story the Bible tells everywhere: God saves a helpless, guilty people by His own strength, and sends good news of it running to the ends of the earth.

context.literary: A chain of oracles bound by repeated imperatives, look, listen, awake, awake, clothe yourself, depart, building toward the herald's announcement in 52:7-10 and the summons to leave Babylon behind in 52:11-12, immediately before the fourth Servant Song.
context.historical: Addressed to exiles in Babylon who needed both comfort, God has not forgotten Abraham's line, and courage, it is time to leave captivity, purified, carrying the temple vessels home, a movement historically fulfilled under Cyrus and the returns led by Zerubbabel and Ezra.
context.canonical: Draws together creation, Rahab and the sea, exodus imagery, the road through the depths, and covenant promise, Abraham and Sarah, into a single sweep, then hands off directly into the fourth Servant Song, the theological summit of the whole book.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To comfort exiles tempted to despair by rooting their hope in God's proven pattern, from one man to a multitude, from bondage to a road through the sea, and to summon them to wake up, trust, and prepare to leave captivity behind, purified and unafraid.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage narrates promises of comfort and deliverance while also prescribing a response, awake, listen, do not fear, depart, purify yourselves; the imperatives are addressed to the original exiles and, by extension, to every reader tempted to stay asleep in captivity to fear or sin.

debates.question: Is the cup of wrath removed from Zion in 51:22 the same cup borne by the Servant in chapter 53?

views.label: A distinct, historical removal
views.case: In its immediate context this is about the end of Babylonian exile, a specific historical judgment lifted after seventy years.

views.label: A pointer to the deeper removal accomplished at the cross
views.case: The pattern, a cup of wrath drained by the guilty then handed to another, anticipates how the Servant will drink the cup His people deserved, language Jesus Himself uses of His own passion, Matthew 26:39.

debates.bearing: Both are true without contradiction; the historical return from exile is a real, lesser fulfillment that points forward to the final, greater removal of wrath accomplished when Christ drank the cup His people deserved.

study.originalLanguages: Zeroa, arm, binds creation, exodus, and salvation into one image of God's active strength, from cutting Rahab to pieces to bearing the arm before all nations. Besorah, good news, roots the herald's beautiful feet in the very word that becomes gospel, the good news that God reigns and has redeemed His people.

typology.type: The arm of the LORD, awakened and bared, 51:9 and 52:10
typology.fulfillment: Unveiled fully in the Suffering Servant of 53:1, and ultimately in Christ crucified and risen.

typology.type: The cup of the LORD's fury removed from Zion's hand, 51:22
typology.fulfillment: The cup Jesus drinks in Gethsemane and on the cross, so it need never be drunk again by those in Him.

typology.type: The herald with beautiful feet proclaiming good news, 52:7
typology.fulfillment: Every gospel messenger, and supremely Christ, the good news embodied, Romans 10:15.

study.christ: The arm of the LORD that split the sea and cut Rahab to pieces is the same arm unveiled in the Suffering Servant, who is Christ; Isaiah 53:1 picks up directly where 52:10 leaves off. The herald's beautiful feet, quoted by Paul in Romans 10:15, describe every messenger of the gospel, but find their fullest sense in Christ Himself, who is the good news that God reigns. And the cup of fury removed from Zion's hand in 51:22 finds its ultimate answer at Gethsemane, where Jesus asks that the cup pass from Him, yet drinks it fully so His people never must.
study.god: God keeps covenant across centuries, from one man, Abraham, to a multiplied people, and from bondage in Egypt to a road through the sea. He judges sin justly, Zion drank a real cup of real wrath, yet He is free to remove that same cup and hand it to the oppressor instead, because His justice will be fully satisfied in another, the Servant of chapter 53.

crossReferences.note: Paul quotes Isaiah 52:7 of those who preach the gospel of Christ, how beautiful are the feet.
Abraham as father of all who believe, the pattern of the one becoming many by faith, echoed in 51:2.
Jesus asks that the cup pass from Him, the cup of wrath He will drink so His people need not.
Babylon is finally given a cup of wrath, the reversal announced in 51:22-23.

study.oneStory: God's pattern never changes: He takes one, Abraham, and makes a multitude; He tears a road through impossible water; He removes a cup of wrath His people had rightly earned. Every strand of this passage runs forward to the arm of the LORD finally and fully revealed in the crucified and risen Christ, the good news whose feet are beautiful because He is Himself the salvation He announces.

quiz.q: Why does Isaiah point Israel back to Abraham and Sarah in 51:1-2?

options.text: To show God's proven pattern of multiplying the one into many, grounding hope for a nation reduced to a remnant.
options.note: The rock and quarry image recalls how God made a whole nation from one man.

options.text: To remind Israel that Abraham never sinned.
options.note: The point is God's multiplying power, not Abraham's sinlessness.

options.text: To prove that Israel no longer needs God's help.
options.note: This overreads the appeal to God's proven power; the passage is precisely a call to continued trust in God's help, not independence from it.

options.text: To explain the geography of the promised land.
options.note: The passage is about God's pattern of grace, not geography.

quiz.q: What is the significance of the cup of the LORD's fury in 51:17-23?

options.text: Zion drained it to the dregs in real judgment, and God announces He will remove it and hand it to her tormentors instead.
It is a cup of blessing Zion had refused to drink.
options.note: It is explicitly the cup of wrath and staggering, not blessing.

options.text: It shows that Zion's judgment will never end.
options.note: The passage announces the judgment's end, not its continuation.

options.text: It refers only to a future judgment on Babylon, with no bearing on Zion.
options.note: Babylon does receive judgment, but the cup is first and explicitly removed from Zion's own hand.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament use Isaiah 52:7, how beautiful are the feet?

options.text: Paul quotes it in Romans 10:15 of those who preach the gospel of Christ.
options.note: Paul applies the verse directly to gospel messengers.

options.text: It is quoted about the feet of the twelve apostles only.
options.note: Apostles are certainly included among gospel messengers, but Paul applies the verse broadly to all who preach the gospel, not a closed list of twelve.

options.text: It has no New Testament citation.
It is used to describe the feet washed at the Last Supper.
options.note: That is a different episode, John 13; this verse is about gospel proclamation.

quiz.q: What connects the arm of the LORD in 51:9-10 to the Servant in Isaiah 53?

options.text: Isaiah 52:10's bared arm leads directly into 53:1's question, to whom has the arm of the LORD been revealed, identifying the Servant as that arm.
options.note: The two passages are joined in sequence, tying creation and exodus power to the Servant's revelation.

options.text: They are unrelated images from different sections of the book.
options.note: They are deliberately joined across the chapter break.

options.text: The arm refers only to Cyrus, Israel's Persian deliverer.
options.note: Cyrus is a genuine, earlier instrument of God's arm in Isaiah 45, but this specific arm imagery culminates in the Servant of 53:1, not a foreign king.

options.text: The arm symbolizes Israel's own military strength.
options.note: It is explicitly the LORD's arm, His own saving strength, not Israel's.

questions: Israel was told to look back to Abraham, one person God multiplied into a nation. What small, seemingly insignificant beginning in your life might God be planning to multiply?
The cup of God's wrath was real, drained to the dregs, before it was removed. Where do you need to remember that grace does not deny the reality of judgment, it answers it?
The passage repeatedly commands awake, awake to a people numbed by suffering. What is God calling you to wake up to and stop sleeping through?
The herald's feet are called beautiful simply because of the news they carry. Who in your life needs you to carry good news to them this week?

Zion is told to leave Babylon purified, touching no unclean thing, yet not fleeing in haste, for the LORD goes before and behind. How does knowing God surrounds your obedience change how you leave behind what has held you captive?

translationNotes.note: Where BSB prints an em dash before the LORD of Hosts is His name, it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
Where BSB prints an em dash before the cup that makes men stagger, it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
Rahab here is a poetic name for chaotic sea-power, sometimes read as a cipher for Egypt, not the Rahab of Jericho in Joshua 2, a different word in Hebrew.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via makor-scripture toolkit for Isaiah 51:1-52:12. Two em dashes rendered as commas per house style, noted in translationNotes.
